metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Calculus 12 introduces students to the foundational concepts of differential and integral calculus — limit theory, derivatives, and integrals — and their applications to rates of change, optimization, and area/volume calculations. As a culminating senior mathematics course, it bridges Pre-calculus 12 to first-year university calculus and is required or recommended for admission to mathematics, sciences, and engineering programs. First Peoples perspectives on rates of change, natural cycles, and cumulative processes are woven throughout.
metadata.general_description: A senior course introducing differential and integral calculus — limits, derivatives, and integrals — and their applications to rates of change, optimization, area, and volume. Required or recommended for post-secondary STEM and mathematics programs.

big_ideas.statement: The concept of a limit is foundational to calculus.
big_ideas.elaboration: The limit — the value a function approaches as its input approaches a given value — is the mathematical foundation of both derivatives and integrals. Understanding limits from tables, graphs, and algebraically is the gateway to calculus.

big_ideas.statement: Differential calculus develops the concept of instantaneous rate of change.
big_ideas.elaboration: The derivative — defined as the limit of the difference quotient — measures the instantaneous rate of change of a function at any point. It has profound applications in physics, engineering, economics, and biology.

big_ideas.statement: Integral calculus develops the concept of determining a product involving a continuously changing quantity over an interval.
big_ideas.elaboration: The definite integral — defined as the limit of a Riemann sum — measures the accumulation of a continuously changing quantity over an interval, with applications to area, volume, distance, and more.

big_ideas.statement: Derivatives and integrals are inversely related.
big_ideas.elaboration: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus reveals a profound and beautiful inverse relationship between differentiation and integration — the two central operations of calculus.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies to solve puzzles and play games; explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and tools; estimate reasonably and demonstrate fluent, flexible thinking about number; model with mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively with curiosity.
i_can_statements: I can develop and apply thinking strategies to evaluate limits, derivatives, and integrals.
I can use graphing technology to explore limits, continuity, derivatives, and antiderivatives.
I can estimate instantaneous rates of change and accumulated quantities from graphs and tables.
I can model real-world rates of change and accumulation problems using calculus.

competencies.raw: Develop conceptual understanding through play, story, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore and illustrate mathematical concepts; apply flexible and strategic approaches to solve problems; solve with persistence and positive disposition; engage in place-based problem-solving.
i_can_statements: I can develop deep conceptual understanding of limits, derivatives, and integrals through inquiry.
I can visualize the relationship between a function and its derivative graphically.
I can apply differentiation and integration rules flexibly, including chain rule and substitution.
I can solve optimization and related rates problems systematically and with persistence.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions in multiple ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary in discussions; take risks when offering ideas.
i_can_statements: I can explain and justify limit evaluations, differentiation steps, and integration processes clearly.
I can represent calculus concepts graphically, numerically, analytically, and verbally.
I can use precise calculus notation: lim, d/dx, f'(x), ∫, dx, and the fundamental theorem.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts with each other and personal interests; use mistakes as opportunities to advance learning; incorporate First Peoples worldviews and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my calculus reasoning and identify conceptual connections between limits, derivatives, and integrals.
I can connect calculus to physics (velocity, acceleration), biology (population growth), and economics (marginal analysis).
I can incorporate First Peoples perspectives on natural rates of change and cumulative processes into mathematical discussions.

areas.name: Functions Review and Preparation
areas.raw: Parent functions; piecewise functions; inverse trigonometric functions.
topics: Review of function families: polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric
Piecewise functions: definition, graphing, and evaluating limits at boundary points
Inverse trigonometric functions: arcsin, arccos, arctan — domain, range, and graphs
Absolute value functions and their behaviour

areas.name: Limits and Continuity
areas.raw: Limits from tables, graphs, and algebraically; one-sided and two-sided limits; end behaviour; continuity; intermediate value theorem.
topics: Intuitive definition of a limit
Evaluating limits from tables and graphs
Evaluating limits algebraically: direct substitution, factoring, rationalization
One-sided limits: left-hand and right-hand limits
Limits that do not exist: oscillation, jump discontinuity, vertical asymptote
Limits at infinity and end behaviour: horizontal asymptotes
Continuity: definition, types of discontinuity (removable, jump, infinite)
Intermediate Value Theorem: existence of roots and solutions

areas.name: Differential Calculus
areas.raw: History and definition of derivative; rate of change; differentiation rules; transcendental functions; higher order and implicit differentiation; applications.
topics: Average rate of change vs. instantaneous rate of change
Definition of the derivative: f'(x) = lim[h→0] (f(x+h)-f(x))/h
Derivative notation: f'(x), dy/dx, Df(x)
Differentiability and continuity: relationship and counterexamples
Differentiation rules: constant, power, sum/difference, constant multiple
Product rule and quotient rule
Chain rule: composite function differentiation
Derivatives of transcendental functions: sin, cos, tan, e^x, ln(x)
Higher order derivatives: f''(x), f'''(x), and interpretations
Implicit differentiation
Applications of derivatives: increasing/decreasing, local max/min (first derivative test)
Concavity and inflection points (second derivative test)
Graph sketching using derivatives: complete curve analysis
Optimization: finding extreme values in applied contexts
Related rates: problems involving rates of change in geometric contexts
Newton's method (optional): numerical root finding

areas.name: Integral Calculus
areas.raw: Definitions and notation; approximation methods; Fundamental Theorem of Calculus; integration methods; applications.
topics: Antiderivatives: definition and basic antiderivative rules
Indefinite integrals: notation ∫f(x)dx, constant of integration C
Riemann sums: left, right, and midpoint approximations
Trapezoidal rule: numerical integration approximation
Definite integral: definition as limit of Riemann sum, area interpretation
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: Part 1 (differentiation of integrals) and Part 2 (evaluation)
Evaluating definite integrals using antiderivatives
Integration by substitution (u-substitution)
Integration by parts (optional enrichment)
Applications: area between curves, volume of solids of revolution (optional), average value of a function
Differential equations: simple separable equations and slope fields
First Peoples connections: traditional accumulation knowledge — fisheries, resource use over time
